findings.title: Pin and verify the downloaded installer
findings.whyItBreaks: Dockerfile:3 downloads and executes whatever content the URL serves during each image build. A compromised or changed upstream artifact can execute arbitrary code in the build and makes source-identical builds non-reproducible.

findings.title: Pin and verify the installer before executing it
findings.whyItBreaks: Every image build downloads and executes mutable network content without a version, checksum, signature, or trusted artifact source. A compromised or changed endpoint can execute arbitrary code in the build environment and alter produced images.

findings.title: Fail the build when the installer download fails
findings.whyItBreaks: Alpine's `/bin/sh -c` reports the exit status of the final `sh` pipeline command. If curl fails before writing content, `sh` receives empty input and exits successfully, so the image is built without the intended installation.

findings.title: Pin and verify the remote installer
findings.whyItBreaks: Every build downloads and executes whatever https://example.com/install.sh serves at that moment. A compromised or changed endpoint can inject arbitrary code into the build image without any repository change or integrity verification.

findings.title: Preserve the missing-key error
findings.whyItBreaks: LoadOrDefault ignores the ErrMissing returned by Load and returns "" for both a missing key and a key explicitly stored with an empty value. Its exported API therefore removes the caller's ability to distinguish absent data from valid empty data.

findings.title: Preserve the missing-key error in the exported helper
findings.whyItBreaks: Load returns ErrMissing for an absent key, but this new exported helper discards it and returns "". A caller cannot distinguish missing data from a key intentionally stored with an empty value.

findings.title: Preserve missing-key errors in LoadOrDefault
findings.whyItBreaks: The new exported helper discards Load's error, so an absent key becomes indistinguishable from an existing key with a legitimate empty-string value.

findings.title: Wait for result sends before returning
findings.whyItBreaks: The new untracked goroutines let Run return before sending results. A caller can close its caller-owned results channel after Run returns, causing a goroutine to panic on send; callers also no longer know that all results were delivered at return.

findings.title: Wait for jobs before returning
findings.whyItBreaks: Each result send now runs in an untracked goroutine, so Run can return before any result is sent. A caller that closes results after Run returns can trigger a send-on-closed-channel panic, and consumers can observe incomplete results.

findings.title: Wait for all result sends before returning
findings.whyItBreaks: Each changed send now runs in an untracked goroutine, so Run can return while sends are pending. A caller that closes results after Run returns can trigger a send-on-closed-channel panic; an absent receiver also leaves one blocked goroutine per job.

findings.title: Restore the admin requirement for record deletion
findings.whyItBreaks: A session with isAdmin=false and a matching tenantId now passes canDeleteRecord. This bypasses the prior admin-only deletion authorization and permits any tenant member to delete another record in that tenant.

findings.title: Require admin status and matching tenant to delete records
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed `||` predicate returns true for a non-admin in the record's tenant and for an admin from another tenant, whereas the prior predicate required both conditions. This exported deletion-authorization decision therefore permits unauthorized deletions.

findings.title: Require both admin status and tenant ownership for deletion
findings.whyItBreaks: A non-admin session for tenant A now passes for any record in tenant A (`false || true`), and an admin from tenant A passes for a record in tenant B (`true || false`). The previous predicate required both conditions, so this change bypasses the deletion authorization boundary.

findings.title: Propagate invalid-settings errors instead of defaulting
findings.whyItBreaks: The new catch discards JSON parse and field-validation failures. updateFontSize then saves defaults with the requested size, overwriting malformed stored settings rather than preserving the prior failure behavior.

findings.title: Propagate invalid-settings errors
findings.whyItBreaks: Malformed JSON or an invalid settings field now becomes a valid-looking default Settings value. updateFontSize then changes its font size and saves it, so input such as "{" overwrites the stored configuration with {"theme":"light","fontSize":16}; previously parsing threw and save was never called.

findings.title: Preserve invalid-settings errors before saving
findings.whyItBreaks: The new catch converts JSON and schema-validation failures into defaults. updateFontSize then serializes and saves those defaults, where the previous code propagated the error without saving.

findings.title: Use the requested branch commit for report artifacts
findings.whyItBreaks: buildReport sets builtFrom from build.ref(branch), but now fetches artifacts using build.ref("verified"). If those refs differ, the returned report associates artifacts from the verified commit with the requested branch commit.

findings.title: Keep report artifacts aligned with builtFrom
findings.whyItBreaks: When the requested branch ref differs from "verified", the returned report exposes the requested branch commit in builtFrom but fetches artifacts from the verified commit. Consumers cannot determine which commit produced the listed artifacts.

findings.title: Use the requested branch commit for report artifacts
findings.whyItBreaks: buildReport resolves the requested branch into `commit` and returns it as `builtFrom`, but fetches artifacts from the separate `verified` ref. When those refs differ, the report pairs one commit with another commit's artifacts.

findings.title: Respect positive limits
findings.whyItBreaks: For any positive limit smaller than all.length, recentEntries now returns every entry. renderFeed passes 20 and copies page.length items into a 20-slot array; with more than 20 entries, JavaScript extends that array and renderFeed returns more than 20 entries.

findings.title: Apply the requested positive limit
findings.whyItBreaks: For any positive limit smaller than all.length, the changed return at src/feed.ts:5 returns every reversed entry. The in-file consumer src/feed.ts:9 passes 20, and its loop writes all returned entries into the output array, expanding the rendered feed beyond 20 entries.

findings.title: Honor limit for positive requests
findings.whyItBreaks: For any positive limit smaller than all.length, recentEntries returns every entry instead of at most limit. renderFeed passes 20 and then writes every returned item into its 20-item page, extending the output beyond 20 entries.

findings.title: Report the number of emitted entries
findings.whyItBreaks: Filtering stale entries removes their rows, but the header still uses entries.length. A manifest with one live and one stale entry declares '# entry-count: 2' while containing one entry row.

findings.title: Count only rendered entries in the manifest header
findings.whyItBreaks: The new filter omits stale entries from rows, but the changed function still passes entries.length to headerLines. With one live and one stale entry, the output declares '# entry-count: 2' while emitting one row.

findings.title: Count only emitted manifest entries
findings.whyItBreaks: The new stale-entry filter omits stale records from the manifest rows, but the emitted `# entry-count` header still uses `entries.length`. A manifest containing stale entries therefore declares more records than it emits.

findings.title: Preserve the exported legacy ID formatter
findings.whyItBreaks: Removing FormatLegacyID is a source-compatibility break: external packages importing ids and calling this exported API will fail to compile after upgrading. No replacement API or migration path is included.

findings.title: Preserve the shippingQuote export
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed module no longer exports `shippingQuote`; consumers importing that public symbol from `src/orders` will fail to compile or load after upgrading.

findings.title: Preserve the shippingQuote export
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed hunk removes the exported shippingQuote function. Existing consumers importing it from this module will fail to compile or load after upgrading.

findings.title: Fail closed when stored hashes are malformed
findings.whyItBreaks: `hmac.compare_digest` raises `TypeError` when `stored_hash` is `None`, `bytes`, or a non-ASCII string; the previous equality check returned `False`. The exception bypasses `guard`'s 401 response at `app/token.py:14`.

score.error: critic produced no summary or checked list (likely malformed output)
critic produced no summary or checked list (likely malformed output)

findings.title: Restore execution instead of always throwing
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed body of exported execute(input) always throws "always fails", so callers cannot receive the declared string result or the prior uppercase output.

findings.title: Restore execute’s successful string result
findings.whyItBreaks: The exported execute(input: string): string contract previously returned the uppercased input, but the changed implementation unconditionally throws. Every consumer calling this API now fails instead of receiving a string.

findings.title: Return the transformed input instead of unconditionally throwing
findings.whyItBreaks: Every call to the exported execute function now throws "always fails", so callers cannot receive the string result declared by its signature; this regresses the prior uppercase behavior.

findings.title: Allow eligible viewers to reach the read-only handler
findings.whyItBreaks: The new `req.role === "viewer"` rejection at src/handler.ts:18 causes active viewers below the attempt limit to return "rejected" from handle instead of reaching its "read-only" branch at src/handler.ts:25.

findings.title: Keep viewer requests on the read-only path
findings.whyItBreaks: For an active viewer with fewer than five attempts, isEligible now returns false, so handle returns "rejected" at src/handler.ts:24. The following viewer branch that returns "read-only" is therefore unreachable, changing viewer requests from a supported read-only outcome to rejection.

findings.title: Preserve active viewers' read-only handling
findings.whyItBreaks: The new `req.role === "viewer"` rejection prevents active viewers below the attempt limit from reaching `handle`'s read-only branch at src/handler.ts:25. Requests that previously returned "read-only" now return "rejected".

findings.title: Keep payment eligibility out of draft saving
findings.whyItBreaks: `canProceed` also gates `saveDraft`. A `{ state: "draft", payment: "missing" }` order now returns false and `saveDraft` returns `blocked`, although payment is not required to save a draft.

findings.title: Keep saving drafts independent of payment status
findings.whyItBreaks: The new payment condition makes saveDraft return "blocked" for draft orders with missing payment, even though saving a draft is separate from submitting an order.

findings.title: Keep payment validation out of draft saving
findings.whyItBreaks: The new payment requirement makes an order with state="draft" and payment="missing" fail canProceed. saveDraft consumes that predicate at src/actions.ts:9 and returns "blocked" instead of "saved".

findings.title: Read snapshot files from the published channel commit
findings.whyItBreaks: When a channel head differs from "stable", Snapshot.commit identifies the requested channel revision but Snapshot.files is loaded from stable. Consumers therefore receive an inconsistent snapshot and cannot reconstruct the reported commit.

findings.title: Read files from the selected channel commit
findings.whyItBreaks: For any channel whose head differs from stable, the returned Snapshot reports that channel's commit but contains the file list from stable. Consumers receive internally inconsistent snapshot data.

findings.title: Use the selected commit when collecting snapshot files
findings.whyItBreaks: The snapshot's `commit` is resolved from the requested `channel`, but `files` are read from the hard-coded `stable` ref. Publishing a channel whose head differs from `stable` returns a self-inconsistent snapshot; repositories without `stable` also fail for otherwise valid channels.

findings.title: Honor the new limit parameter
findings.whyItBreaks: `build("item", limit=3)` still returns 10 items because the implementation continues to iterate over hard-coded `max_count = 10`; the new public parameter's observable contract is therefore false.

findings.title: Honor the new limit parameter
findings.whyItBreaks: Calling `build("x", limit=2)` still returns 10 values because the loop reads the unchanged hard-coded `max_count = 10`. The public parameter name promises that it controls the result size.

findings.title: Honor the advertised limit parameter
findings.whyItBreaks: `build(prefix, limit=10)` newly exposes `limit` as a result-affecting parameter, but the body still iterates `range(10)`. Thus `build("x", limit=3)` returns ten values rather than three.

findings.title: Validate positivity in parse_positive_int
findings.whyItBreaks: The renamed public helper promises a positive integer, but `int(value)` accepts `0` and negative inputs. Consequently `charge("-2")` returns `-200`, so callers relying on the advertised validation can create negative charges.

findings.title: Enforce the positive-integer contract
findings.whyItBreaks: parse_positive_int('0') returns 0 and parse_positive_int('-1') returns -1, despite its public name promising a positive integer. charge therefore produces zero or negative cents rather than rejecting invalid amounts.

findings.title: Reject non-positive values in parse_positive_int
findings.whyItBreaks: parse_positive_int("-1") returns -1 and parse_positive_int("0") returns 0, contradicting its public positivity contract. charge("-1") consequently returns -100 cents.

findings.title: Preserve the caller's DataFrame
findings.whyItBreaks: The prior implementation copied df before applying mappings. The changed assignments write to df directly and return that same object, so callers retaining the input now observe overwritten values.

findings.title: Preserve the caller DataFrame instead of mutating it
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed assignments update the function argument directly. Callers that retain or reuse df will observe its values modified, whereas the base implementation returned a modified copy.

findings.title: Preserve the input DataFrame
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed assignments now write directly through df.loc and return df. Previously the function copied df and modified only that copy, so callers retaining the input now observe unexpected source-data mutation.

findings.title: Enforce the positive-integer contract
findings.whyItBreaks: `parse_positive_int("-5")` returns -5, and the in-file consumer `charge` at `src/parse.rs:6` multiplies it into a negative charge. The newly introduced public name promises a positive result.

findings.title: Enforce positivity in parse_positive_int
findings.whyItBreaks: The new public name promises a positive integer, but `"-1".parse()` succeeds and returns -1. Consequently `charge("-1")` returns -100, so callers relying on the renamed contract can process a negative charge.

findings.title: Enforce positivity in parse_positive_int
findings.whyItBreaks: `parse_positive_int("-2")` returns `-2`, contradicting the newly introduced positive-value contract; `charge` consequently returns `-200`.

findings.title: Borrow items instead of moving it into dbg!
findings.whyItBreaks: `dbg!(items)` consumes the `Vec<String>`; the following `items.len()` then borrows a moved value and Rust emits E0382.

findings.title: Avoid moving items into dbg! before using it
findings.whyItBreaks: `dbg!(items)` moves the owned `Vec<String>` and its statement result is dropped. The following `items.len()` therefore borrows a moved value, producing E0382.

findings.title: Borrow `items` in the debug macro
findings.whyItBreaks: `dbg!(items)` takes ownership of the `Vec<String>`, so the following `items.len()` uses a moved value and produces Rust error E0382.

score.error: critic produced no summary or checked list (likely malformed output)

findings.title: Compute changed paths from the returned base revision
findings.whyItBreaks: `fromRevision` is resolved from `pinnedTag`, but the changed line reads paths from independently resolved `latest`. The documented `Changeset` contract at `src/changeset.ts:7-8` says consumers replay paths on `fromRevision`, so distinct revisions produce an incoherent changeset.

findings.title: Compute paths from the advertised base revision
findings.whyItBreaks: `fromRevision` is the resolved pinned tag, and the exported contract says `changedPaths` was computed against it. The changed line instead reads paths using independently resolved `latest`; when they differ, consumers receive paths for `latest` alongside `fromRevision`.

findings.title: Compute changed paths from the pinned revision
findings.whyItBreaks: When pinnedTag resolves to a revision other than "latest", changedPaths describes changes from latest while fromRevision remains the pinned revision. Consumers replaying the changeset against fromRevision therefore apply a path set computed against the wrong base.

findings.title: Update queries to use contact_email
findings.whyItBreaks: After this migration, users.email no longer exists, but src/query.sql still executes `SELECT email FROM users`; upgraded deployments will fail that query with a missing-column error.

findings.title: Update queries to use the renamed email column
findings.whyItBreaks: After this migration, `users.email` no longer exists, but `src/query.sql:1` still executes `SELECT email FROM users`; deployments that apply the migration will fail this query with a missing-column error.

findings.title: Update queries to use the renamed email column
findings.whyItBreaks: After this migration, `users.email` no longer exists, but `src/query.sql:1` still executes `SELECT email FROM users`, causing a column-not-found SQL error on upgraded databases.

findings.title: Preserve the missing billing-key failure
findings.whyItBreaks: When `BILLING_API_KEY` is absent, the exported `makeClient` previously threw an explicit configuration error; it now returns a usable-looking client with a hard-coded value. Callers can no longer distinguish missing deployment configuration from a valid configured client.

findings.title: Restore runtime billing-key configuration
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed exported makeClient function hard-codes a bk_live_ credential and no longer reads BILLING_API_KEY or rejects its absence. Consumers now receive a client with the repository credential even when deployment configuration is missing or invalid.

findings.title: Restore environment-based billing credentials
findings.whyItBreaks: This commits a live-looking billing key to source control and ignores BILLING_API_KEY entirely. Every deployment now uses the same exposed credential, so rotation and environment-specific billing credentials cannot take effect; an unset configuration also no longer fails safely.

findings.title: Restore the admin authorization check
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed guard returns "forbidden" for admins and reaches `db.delete(project.id)` for non-admin users when the project is archived, creating an authorization bypass for destructive deletion.

findings.title: Restore admin-only purge authorization
findings.whyItBreaks: For an archived project, `user.isAdmin=false` passes both guards and reaches `db.delete`, while `user.isAdmin=true` returns `forbidden`. This reverses the prior authorization rule for a destructive operation.

findings.title: Restore the admin authorization check
findings.whyItBreaks: `user.isAdmin === true` now returns "forbidden", while a non-admin with an archived project reaches `src/projects.ts:14` and triggers `db.delete`. This reverses the prior authorization boundary and permits unauthorized deletion.

findings.title: Restore startup defaults when the settings file is absent
findings.whyItBreaks: load_settings returns None for a nonexistent path. The changed startup body immediately indexes settings["workers"], raising TypeError instead of returning the previous default worker count.

findings.title: Restore handling for a missing settings file
findings.whyItBreaks: load_settings returns None for a nonexistent path, and the removed fallback means startup indexes None at line 14 rather than returning the prior default worker count of 1.

findings.title: Restore defaults when the settings file is absent
findings.whyItBreaks: load_settings returns None when the path does not exist (app/config.py:6-7). The removed fallback means startup indexes None and raises TypeError instead of returning the prior default worker configuration.

findings.title: Propagate gateway submission failures
findings.whyItBreaks: When Gateway.Submit fails, the exported Charge function only logs the error and returns nil. Consumers therefore receive a successful result for a failed charge.

findings.title: Propagate gateway submission failures
findings.whyItBreaks: When Gateway.Submit fails, Charge now only logs the error and returns nil. Its exported API therefore makes a failed payment indistinguishable from a successful one, allowing callers to mark or act on an unpaid charge as successful.

findings.title: Propagate gateway submission failures
findings.whyItBreaks: When Gateway.Submit returns an error, the changed branch logs it and then falls through to `return nil`, collapsing a failed charge into a successful exported Charge call. External callers can therefore proceed as though payment succeeded.

findings.title: Keep tenant IDs in settings cache keys
findings.whyItBreaks: Both settingsFor('tenant-a', 'theme', ...) and settingsFor('tenant-b', 'theme', ...) now use settings:theme. Once tenant A populates it, tenant B receives tenant A's value without calling its store.

findings.title: Preserve invalidate's tenant-aware public signature
findings.whyItBreaks: This exported function previously accepted (tenantId, name). Existing TypeScript consumers fail to compile after upgrade; JavaScript consumers still passing two arguments use tenantId as name, so they delete settings:<tenantId> rather than the requested setting's cache entry.

findings.title: Include tenantId in the settings cache key
findings.whyItBreaks: After tenant A caches `name`, a call for tenant B with the same name hits `settings:${name}` and returns tenant A's value without calling `store.fetch(tenantB, name)`. This changes tenant-scoped settings into globally shared values.

findings.title: Keep tenant ID in the settings cache key
findings.whyItBreaks: After tenant A fetches `name`, `settingsFor` stores it under `settings:<name>`. A request for the same name from tenant B hits that entry at src/settings.ts:13 and returns tenant A's value without calling `store.fetch(tenantB, name)`, exposing or applying another tenant's setting.

findings.title: Serialize reservations before awaiting persistence
findings.whyItBreaks: The await yields after reading stock but before updating it. With one unit loaded, two concurrent reserve calls both read 1, both save 0, and both return true, so the inventory grants two reservations for one unit.

findings.title: Serialize reservations before awaiting persistence
findings.whyItBreaks: Two concurrent reserve calls for a SKU with stock=1 can both read remaining=1 before either save resolves. Both persist 0, both return true, and two reservations are accepted for one item.

findings.title: Preserve the exported Inventory API or version the breaking change
findings.whyItBreaks: Existing consumers using new Inventory() now fail because Store is required, and callers using the synchronous boolean result from reserve now receive a Promise<boolean>. This is an immediate source-compatibility break for the exported class.

findings.title: Keep construction compatible with existing Inventory callers
findings.whyItBreaks: Inventory previously had an implicit zero-argument constructor. Existing TypeScript consumers using new Inventory() now fail to compile; existing JavaScript consumers construct an instance with store undefined and reserve() throws when stock is available.

findings.title: Avoid changing reserve from a boolean to a Promise
findings.whyItBreaks: Previously, callers could branch directly on reserve(sku). Existing JavaScript callers now receive a truthy Promise, so `if (inventory.reserve(sku))` proceeds even when stock is empty; TypeScript callers expecting boolean no longer compile.

findings.title: Serialize reservations across the persistence await
findings.whyItBreaks: With one unit loaded, two concurrent reserve calls both read remaining as 1 before either awaited save resolves. Both save 0 and both return true, granting two reservations from one unit.

findings.title: Keep retry delays in milliseconds
findings.whyItBreaks: `retry` still calls `sleep(backoff(a) * 1000)`. The first retry delay changes from 1,000 ms to 1,000,000 ms (about 16m40s), so transient failures effectively stall retry workflows.

findings.title: Update retry to consume millisecond backoff values
findings.whyItBreaks: backoff(0) now returns 1,000 milliseconds, but retry() still calls sleep(backoff(a) * 1000). A first retry therefore waits 1,000,000 ms instead of 1 second; capped retries wait about 8.3 hours instead of 30 seconds.

findings.title: Stop multiplying millisecond backoff in retry
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed helper now returns milliseconds. retry() still calls sleep(backoff(a) * 1000) at src/jobs/retry.ts:12, making retry waits 1,000× too long: attempt 0 waits 1,000,000 ms rather than 1,000 ms.

findings.title: Propagate the close failure
findings.whyItBreaks: The new `defer f.Close()` discards a close error. Before this change, Save returned `f.Close()` after a successful flush, so callers could detect a failed final write or close; it now returns nil.

findings.title: Propagate the file close error
findings.whyItBreaks: The new `defer f.Close()` discards Close's return value. Previously, after a successful Flush, Save returned `f.Close()`'s error; it now returns nil when Close fails.

findings.title: Propagate the deferred Close error
findings.whyItBreaks: The previous implementation returned f.Close() errors after a successful flush. Deferring Close without capturing its result discards failures reported while closing the created file, so the exported Save API can return nil despite an unsuccessful final close.

findings.title: Include the final row in each page window
findings.whyItBreaks: `range(start, end - 1)` excludes `end - 1`, so every nonempty page loses its last row; a page size of one always returns an empty window. `render_page` and `summarize` therefore omit rows from their public output.

findings.title: Include the final row in each page
findings.whyItBreaks: Python's range stop is exclusive, so range(start, end - 1) returns rows through end - 2. A one-row page becomes empty, and every other page omits its final row; render_page and summarize therefore produce incomplete output.

findings.title: Include the final row in each page window
findings.whyItBreaks: range(start, end - 1) excludes index end - 1, so a full page of size N returns N-1 rows and a one-row final page returns no rows. render_page and summarize therefore silently omit rows.

findings.title: Restore the TTL comparison
findings.whyItBreaks: Fresh entries have an age near zero, so a positive TTL causes get() to delete them and return null. After the TTL elapses, get() returns the expired value.

findings.title: Avoid splicing while iterating jobs
findings.whyItBreaks: Splicing a completed job shifts the following job into the current index, while forEach advances past it. Two adjacent completed jobs therefore leave the second job in the returned array.

findings.title: Return fresh entries and expire stale entries
findings.whyItBreaks: Entries younger than ttlMs are deleted and return null, while entries older than ttlMs are returned indefinitely. This reverses the public TtlCache contract and can serve stale values.

findings.title: Remove every completed job
findings.whyItBreaks: Splicing during forEach shifts the next job into the current index, but forEach advances to the next index. For [{done:true}, {done:true}], the second completed job remains in the returned queue.

findings.title: Restore the TTL validity comparison
findings.whyItBreaks: Fresh entries (age <= ttlMs) are deleted and return null, while expired entries return their stale value indefinitely.

findings.title: Remove all adjacent completed jobs
findings.whyItBreaks: Splicing during forEach shifts the next job into the current index, but forEach advances past it. For [{done:true}, {done:true}], one completed job remains.

findings.title: Restore numeric descending score ordering
findings.whyItBreaks: Array.sort() without a comparator orders numbers as strings. For scores [8, 100, 9] and n=2, the reversed order is [9, 8, 100], so the cutoff becomes 8; preserving input order then returns players scoring 8 and 100, excluding the player scoring 9.

findings.title: Restore numeric score ordering
findings.whyItBreaks: Array.sort() without a comparator compares numeric scores as strings. For input ordered as [{score:8}, {score:100}, {score:9}] with n=2, rankScores returns [9,8,100], making the cutoff 8; topPlayers then retains all players and slice(0,2) returns scores 8 and 100, incorrectly excluding score 9.

findings.title: Restore numeric score comparison before reversing
findings.whyItBreaks: Array.sort() without a comparator sorts numbers as strings. For players ordered with scores 9, 2, and 10, rankScores returns [9, 2, 10]; with n=1 the cutoff becomes 9, so topPlayers returns the 9-point player and omits the 10-point player.

findings.title: Restore constant-time webhook signature comparison
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed `==` comparison can reveal how much of an attacker-supplied signature matches the expected HMAC through comparison timing. `handle()` uses `verify()` as its authorization gate before invoking `processor(payload)`, so a successful signature forgery permits webhook processing.

findings.title: Restore constant-time signature comparison
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed `==` comparison at line 10 compares an attacker-supplied signature before `handle` invokes `processor` at app/webhook.py:14. This can expose HMAC-prefix timing differences across repeated requests.

findings.title: Use a constant-time comparison for webhook signatures
findings.whyItBreaks: String equality may return after the first mismatching character, exposing signature-prefix timing differences. An attacker able to repeatedly invoke this webhook could use those differences to forge the HMAC and reach the authenticated processor path.

findings.title: Keep ledger and balance updates in one transaction
findings.whyItBreaks: The transaction commits after `_record_ledger`; either subsequent balance update can fail after the ledger entry is durable, leaving balances unchanged or partially updated.

findings.title: Keep balance updates inside the transaction
findings.whyItBreaks: The transaction commits after `_record_ledger`, before either balance update. If `_apply_balances` fails, the ledger entry remains committed without both matching balance changes; previously all three writes were in one transaction.

findings.title: Keep all transfer writes in one transaction
findings.whyItBreaks: The transaction commits after `_record_ledger`; the changed call to `_apply_balances` is outside that transaction. If either balance update fails, the ledger entry remains committed without matching balance changes, and a failure between the two updates can leave only the debit applied.

findings.title: Preserve UTC when deriving billing days
findings.whyItBreaks: This replaces UTC date extraction with local-time getters. At 2024-01-01T00:30:00Z, a host in America/Los_Angeles returns 2023-12-31 rather than 2024-01-01, so billing-day assignment varies by deployment timezone.

findings.title: Preserve UTC when deriving the billing day
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed return expression uses local-time getters. An instant such as 2026-01-01T00:30:00Z is classified as 2025-12-31 on UTC-08 hosts, unlike the previous UTC-based implementation.

findings.title: Keep billing-day calculation in UTC
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed implementation uses host-local calendar getters instead of the previous UTC getters. The same instant can therefore be assigned to a different billing day on non-UTC hosts; `sameBillingDay` inherits that altered classification.

findings.title: Include tenant in the memoization key
findings.whyItBreaks: After tenant A/account X populates memo, tenant B/account X returns tenant A's cached Profile without calling fetchProfile. This leaks cross-tenant profile data.

findings.title: Keep tenant in the memoization key
findings.whyItBreaks: Two tenants with the same account value share one memo entry. After tenant A loads account X, a read for tenant B/account X returns tenant A's cached Profile without calling fetchProfile, exposing the wrong tenant's data.

findings.title: Include tenant in the profile cache key
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed account-only key causes a profile cached for `(tenantA, account)` to be returned for `(tenantB, account)`, without invoking `fetchProfile`. This crosses tenant boundaries and can expose another tenant’s data.

findings.title: Preserve missing-key failures in load
findings.whyItBreaks: The exported `load` function now catches and discards its missing-key error, returning `""` instead. This conflates lookup failure with a legitimately stored empty value; `loadAll` at `src/store.ts:12` emits that value as successful data.

findings.title: Preserve missing-key errors from load
findings.whyItBreaks: The new empty catch returns `""` for a missing key, making failure indistinguishable from a stored empty string. `loadAll` at src/store.ts:12 maps those values into a successful result instead of propagating the error.

findings.title: Preserve missing-key errors from load
findings.whyItBreaks: The new catch returns "" for an absent key, making a missing value indistinguishable from a stored empty string. loadAll at src/store.ts:12 includes this fallback in its returned array.

findings.title: Preserve the existing timeoutMs configuration contract
findings.whyItBreaks: Existing TypeScript callers using Config with { maxAttempts, timeoutMs } no longer type-check because timeoutMs was removed and retries/timeout are newly required. JavaScript callers with the old shape get config.timeout === undefined; Date.now() + undefined is NaN, so the timeout guard at line 10 is always false and retries can run past the previously configured deadline.

findings.title: Expose exhausted background retry failures
findings.whyItBreaks: The new exported retryBackground catches every exception and returns void after all attempts or the deadline. Its caller therefore cannot distinguish successful execution from an operation that failed on every attempt, and may treat a failed background operation as completed.

findings.title: Preserve the existing Config shape
findings.whyItBreaks: This exported interface replaces required timeoutMs with timeout and adds required retries. Existing TypeScript callers passing the prior { maxAttempts, timeoutMs } contract no longer compile; JavaScript callers receive deadline=NaN from an undefined timeout and therefore bypass the timeout check.

findings.title: Report terminal failures from retryBackground
findings.whyItBreaks: After every attempt throws, this exported function catches each error and implicitly returns undefined—the same value returned after success. Callers cannot distinguish exhaustion from a completed retry operation.

findings.title: Expose failure when background retries are exhausted
findings.whyItBreaks: The exported `retryBackground` catches every callback exception and returns `void` after retries are exhausted or the deadline passes—the same observable result as a successful callback. Public callers therefore cannot distinguish failure from success.

findings.title: Handle numeric values before reading length
findings.whyItBreaks: `FieldProps.value` now accepts `number`, but `number` has no `length`; `<Field value={123} />` produces `len=undefined`, and the property access is a TypeScript error.

findings.title: Handle numeric values before reading length
findings.whyItBreaks: `FieldProps.value` now accepts `number`, but the changed `value.length` access is invalid on that union, causing TypeScript compilation to fail. A numeric caller also has no defined length semantics.

findings.title: Handle numeric values before reading their length
findings.whyItBreaks: `FieldProps` now permits `number`, but `number` has no `length`; TypeScript rejects this access and JavaScript would render numeric inputs as `len=undefined`.

findings.title: Preserve the git output buffer limit
findings.whyItBreaks: Removing maxBuffer restores Node's 1 MiB default for every git invocation. The patch is read with git diff at line 140, so a PR whose textual diff exceeds 1 MiB makes spawnSync terminate with ENOBUFS; git() throws and runGithub posts a failed review check instead of reviewing the PR.

findings.title: Keep bundle baseSha aligned with the patch base
findings.whyItBreaks: The patch and changed-file set are computed from mergeBase (lines 140-141), but the bundle now reports baseSha. When PR_BASE_SHA is the current base-branch tip after the PR diverged, it differs from mergeBase, so consumers receive a base/head pair describing a different comparison than the supplied patch and changed files. A reviewer following the reported SHAs can inspect unrelated base-branch commits or fail to map findings to the patch.

findings.title: Retain the diff buffer limit
findings.whyItBreaks: `git()` produces the complete patch at line 140. Removing `maxBuffer` restores `spawnSync`'s 1 MiB default, so a PR whose textual diff exceeds that size terminates the git command and the review job posts a failure instead of reviewing the PR.

findings.title: Preserve the Git diff buffer limit
findings.whyItBreaks: spawnSync now uses Node's default 1 MiB maxBuffer. The same helper captures the full PR patch at line 140, so a PR with a diff over 1 MiB causes git to terminate with a max-buffer error and the review fails instead of producing a result. The previous 64 MiB limit supported these diffs.

findings.title: Restore the explicit read-only Codex sandbox
findings.whyItBreaks: Removing `-s read-only` makes `runCodex` inherit Codex's configured/default sandbox. If that policy permits `workspace-write`, model-generated shell commands from the supplied prompt can modify `opts.repoPath`, whereas the previous invocation prevented those writes.

findings.title: Restore the read-only sandbox for review runs
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed args no longer pass `-s read-only`. The subprocess is launched with `opts.repoPath` as its cwd, so a configured/default sandbox that permits workspace writes can modify the repository under review.

findings.title: Keep the review subprocess read-only
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed args no longer pass `-s read-only`. The subprocess is launched with `cwd: opts.repoPath`, so the review agent no longer has the previous enforced non-mutating sandbox boundary and can write to the repository it reviews.

findings.title: Build the review diff against headSha
findings.whyItBreaks: When PR_HEAD_SHA differs from the checkout's HEAD, the bundle advertises PR_HEAD_SHA as its head but supplies a patch and changed-file list for HEAD. Reviews can therefore analyze one commit and post their result to another.

findings.title: Diff from the merge base, not the current base tip
findings.whyItBreaks: When the PR branch diverged before newer commits landed on its base branch, `git diff baseSha HEAD` treats those base-only commits as deletions in the review bundle. `changedFiles` gets the same incorrect scope, so Needlefish can review unrelated files and attempt inline comments that are not part of the PR.

findings.title: Use the configured PR head SHA for the review diff
findings.whyItBreaks: With `PR_HEAD_SHA` set and the checkout's `HEAD` different from that SHA, the bundle now reviews `HEAD` while `postReview` and `postCheck` attach the result to `headSha`. This produces comments and a check for one commit based on another commit's files and patch.

findings.title: Diff against the configured PR head
findings.whyItBreaks: When PR_HEAD_SHA identifies the PR commit but HEAD is a different checkout (such as a GitHub pull-request merge commit), the bundle claims headSha is PR_HEAD_SHA while patch and changedFiles are computed through HEAD. The review can therefore describe merge-result changes while posting against PR_HEAD_SHA.

findings.title: Preserve literal prompt payloads during replacement
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed string replacement values interpret `$` sequences. Content containing `$&` is replaced with the matched placeholder, while `$`` and `$'` inject surrounding prompt text. Because bundle JSON, candidate JSON, and bundle.patch are all interpolated this way before runCodex is called, Codex can receive corrupted review context.

findings.title: Preserve literal prompt payloads
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed string replacement values are interpreted by `String.prototype.replace`: `$&` becomes the matched placeholder and `$``/`$'` inject surrounding prompt text. Consequently, `$` token sequences in serialized bundle data, generated findings, or `bundle.patch` are not passed verbatim to Codex, producing corrupted review or critic prompts. Restore callback replacements so payloads are inserted literally.

findings.title: Preserve dollar signs when interpolating prompt payloads
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed string-replacement arguments are interpreted by JavaScript's String.replace: payload text such as `$&`, `$`` or `$'` expands to match or surrounding prompt text instead of being inserted literally. This corrupts valid patches, bundle JSON, or candidate findings before they are sent to Codex. Callback replacements previously preserved the payload verbatim.

findings.title: Keep fallback review pinned to the analyzed commit
findings.whyItBreaks: When the inline-comment request fails, this fallback omits commit_id. GitHub can then bind the review to the PR's current head rather than the headSha used to construct the reviewed patch. A commit pushed during review() can therefore cause a verdict for the old diff to attach to a newer, unreviewed revision.

findings.title: Keep the analyzed SHA in the fallback review request
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed fallback request omits `commit_id`. If the PR advances after `headSha` is captured, GitHub attaches the fallback review to the latest commit while Needlefish's verdict and check run target the earlier SHA.

findings.title: Preserve invalid-finding errors instead of fabricating defaults
findings.whyItBreaks: Invalid severities, categories, required text fields, and locations are now converted into valid-looking Finding values (including P1, bug, empty file/text, and line 0). The exported normalizeReview maps these entries directly into its result, so consumers cannot distinguish malformed review output from genuine findings.

findings.title: Preserve rejection of malformed findings
findings.whyItBreaks: Invalid findings no longer surface validation failures: invalid severity/category and missing required fields are converted into fallback values such as P1, bug, empty strings, and line 0. Callers of this exported normalizer consequently receive output that appears to be a legitimate Finding instead of an explicit malformed-output error.

findings.title: Retain the strict=false filtering contract
findings.whyItBreaks: Removing the exported second parameter breaks TypeScript callers that pass normalizeReview(raw, false). JavaScript callers have the argument ignored; a null finding then reaches normalizeFinding and throws when it reads raw.severity, whereas the former strict=false path omitted malformed entries.

findings.title: Preserve the exported non-strict review parsing mode
findings.whyItBreaks: Previously, `normalizeReview(raw, false)` skipped malformed findings. The removed parameter means existing callers can no longer request that behavior: malformed object entries are emitted as fallback findings, while `null` entries now throw when `normalizeFinding` reads `raw.severity`. This is a breaking behavior change for the exported parser.

findings.title: Keep an explicit repository for dispatched PR resolution
findings.whyItBreaks: For workflow_dispatch, EVENT_HEAD is empty, so the changed gh pr view calls at lines 38-39 run before the first checkout and have no repository remote to infer. GITHUB_REPOSITORY is not GH_REPO; without -R or GH_REPO, gh cannot identify the target repository. The resulting head/base outputs are unavailable, preventing checkout and review of the requested PR.

findings.title: Retain the repository target for manual PR resolution
findings.whyItBreaks: On workflow_dispatch, EVENT_HEAD is empty and this branch runs before the first checkout. Without -R "$REPO", gh pr view must infer a repository from the runner working directory; a fresh workspace has no checkout or Git remote, so PR ref resolution fails and the manual workflow cannot proceed to the checkout at .github/workflows/review.yml:42.

findings.title: Restore explicit repository when resolving dispatched PR refs
findings.whyItBreaks: For workflow_dispatch, pull_request SHA fields are empty, so execution reaches the changed pre-checkout `gh pr view` calls. Without `-R` or `GH_REPO`, gh cannot infer a repository because no checkout or Git remote exists yet; manual PR reviews therefore cannot resolve their head/base SHAs. The dispatch input is defined at .github/workflows/review.yml:10-14.

findings.title: Reject malformed review output instead of returning an empty review
findings.whyItBreaks: Inputs such as null, {}, or a response missing required arrays previously threw an error; they now normalize to the same empty values as a legitimate no-findings review. Because this exported API removes the only failure signal, callers cannot distinguish invalid reviewer output from a valid clean review and may treat it as a pass.

findings.title: Preserve errors for malformed review output
findings.whyItBreaks: Invalid or incomplete review output previously threw a malformed-output error. The changed fallback logic now returns an indistinguishable empty RawReview, forcing callers of this exported API to treat parser/provider failure as a valid review with no findings.

findings.title: Reject malformed review output instead of returning an empty review
findings.whyItBreaks: This exported function now turns missing or wrong-typed required fields into an apparently valid empty review. Callers can no longer distinguish malformed model output from a legitimate review with no findings or risks.

findings.title: Resolve the requested PR head for manual dispatch
findings.whyItBreaks: On workflow_dispatch, github.event.pull_request is absent, so this falls back to github.ref (the branch or tag selected to run the workflow), not the pr_number input. Manual reviews can therefore run against an unrelated ref rather than the requested PR head.

findings.title: Restore PR-ref resolution for manual dispatches
findings.whyItBreaks: For workflow_dispatch, github.event.pull_request is absent. Changed line 29 therefore checks out github.ref—the branch from which the workflow was dispatched—while changed lines 45-46 pass empty PR_BASE_SHA and PR_HEAD_SHA. The required pr_number only reaches the CLI argument and no longer determines the checkout or diff refs, so manually reviewing a PR from main can review main rather than that PR.

findings.title: Resolve requested PR refs for manual dispatches
findings.whyItBreaks: On workflow_dispatch, github.event.pull_request is absent, so the new checkout ref falls back to github.ref—the branch selected when dispatching—not inputs.pr_number. BASE_REF similarly defaults to main, while the review command at .github/workflows/review.yml:47 receives empty PR_BASE_SHA and PR_HEAD_SHA. A manually supplied PR can therefore be reviewed from the wrong checkout with no resolved PR SHAs.

findings.title: Retain a buffer large enough for review diffs
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed `git` helper now uses spawnSync's default stdout buffer. `runLocal` obtains the complete patch via `git(["diff", baseSha, "HEAD"], cwd)` at src/adapters/local.ts:100; when that patch exceeds the default 1 MiB buffer, git is terminated and the helper throws, preventing the local review. Restore an explicit larger buffer, such as the prior 64 MiB value.

findings.title: Restore capacity for large generated diffs
findings.whyItBreaks: Without maxBuffer, spawnSync uses Node's 1 MiB default. runLocal obtains the entire review input through `git diff` at line 100; a diff exceeding that limit makes spawnSync terminate the Git command and the status guard throws, so the local reviewer cannot review otherwise valid medium-to-large PRs. The previous implementation explicitly supported up to 64 MiB.

findings.title: Restore the Git output buffer limit
findings.whyItBreaks: `spawnSync` defaults `maxBuffer` to 1 MiB. `runLocal` reads the complete `git diff` at line 100, so reviewing a PR whose patch exceeds that size terminates the Git command and throws instead of producing a review. The removed limit was 64 MiB.

findings.title: Keep review failures as failing checks
findings.whyItBreaks: When review() throws, the changed error path reports a neutral completed check. Neutral satisfies GitHub required-check protection, so a review that produced no verdict can allow a PR to pass the required Needlefish check.

findings.title: Post the check to the checked-out commit
findings.whyItBreaks: The change replaces the checked-out HEAD SHA with pr.head.sha. On a standard pull_request Actions run, HEAD may be GitHub's synthetic test-merge commit; when strict required checks are evaluated on that commit, the Needlefish check is instead attached only to the PR head and is absent from the merge commit.

findings.title: Keep review errors as failed checks
findings.whyItBreaks: When review() throws, the catch path posts the required Needlefish check with conclusion "neutral" rather than "failure". GitHub treats neutral check conclusions as non-failing, so a required Needlefish check can pass despite no review result being produced.

findings.title: Keep review errors as failed checks
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed catch path posts the completed check run named "Needlefish" with conclusion "neutral" after review() throws. Neutral is non-blocking for a required GitHub check, so a review that never completed can satisfy a branch rule requiring this check.

findings.title: Restore a trusted checkout for the privileged reviewer
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed workflow checks out the PR head and then runs its `pnpm install` and `src/cli.ts` directly. A same-repository PR can modify package lifecycle metadata or the CLI to execute arbitrary code on the self-hosted runner while `GH_TOKEN` has `pull-requests: write` and `checks: write` permissions.

findings.title: Keep the reviewer checkout pinned to trusted main
findings.whyItBreaks: The PR-head checkout is now the working directory for both `pnpm install` and `src/cli.ts`. A same-repository PR can modify its package manifest, lockfile lifecycle hooks, or `src/cli.ts`; that code then runs on the self-hosted runner with `GH_TOKEN` and the workflow's `pull-requests: write` and `checks: write` permissions. The former separate checkout of `frankekn/needlefish@main` prevented the PR from controlling the reviewer.

findings.title: Restore a trusted tool checkout
findings.whyItBreaks: The job checks out the same-repository PR head at lines 43-47, then executes its src/cli.ts with a write-scoped GH_TOKEN at lines 55-59 on a self-hosted runner (line 23). A PR can modify the CLI or its dependencies and have that code run with the token and runner access.

findings.title: Point the workflow at an available Needlefish CLI
findings.whyItBreaks: The head tree contains only .github/workflows/review.yml and .needlefish/answers.json; it has no package.json, lockfile, node_modules/.bin/tsx, or src/cli.ts. This command therefore targets nonexistent files, so every review job fails instead of posting a review.

findings.title: Preserve canonical severity normalization
findings.whyItBreaks: normalizeReview accepts untyped external output and passes each item to normalizeFinding. A valid semantic severity such as " p0 " or "p0" previously normalized to P0, but the new exact-membership check rejects it and emits P3, silently demoting critical findings.

findings.title: Preserve canonical severity normalization
findings.whyItBreaks: `normalizeReview` maps untyped external findings through this function. Lowercase or padded valid severities (for example, `"p2"` or `" p0 "`) no longer match exact membership and are returned as `P3`, understating the finding.

findings.title: Preserve severity string normalization before validation
findings.whyItBreaks: normalizeReview passes external raw findings to normalizeFinding, which now validates severity exactly. Values previously accepted after normalization, such as " p0 " or "p1", fail the check and are emitted as P3.

findings.title: Keep GitHub credentials out of the Codex subprocess
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed spawn call no longer supplies the environment that removed `GH_TOKEN`, `GITHUB_TOKEN`, and `GITHUB_API_TOKEN`; Node therefore inherits them from the parent process. This function sends its `prompt` directly to the Codex subprocess via stdin, allowing prompt-controlled agent behavior to access inherited credentials.

findings.title: Keep GitHub tokens out of the Codex subprocess
findings.whyItBreaks: Removing the explicit `env` option makes `spawnSync` inherit `process.env`, including GH_TOKEN, GITHUB_TOKEN, and GITHUB_API_TOKEN. Codex runs with the reviewed repository as its working directory, so untrusted repository content can influence a process that now receives CI GitHub credentials.

findings.title: Restore GitHub-token sanitization for the Codex child
findings.whyItBreaks: Removing the `env` option makes `spawnSync` inherit `process.env`, exposing `GH_TOKEN`, `GITHUB_TOKEN`, and `GITHUB_API_TOKEN` to the Codex process. The prior revision explicitly removed these credentials before launching the prompt- and repository-influenced child process.

findings.title: Restore the fork trust gate for the self-hosted runner
findings.whyItBreaks: Fork PRs now enter this job, which checks out the contributor-controlled head SHA and executes `pnpm install` plus `tsx src/cli.ts` on a self-hosted runner. An attacker can supply install hooks or review code to execute arbitrary commands on that runner, enabling runner persistence and access to its network and any resident credentials.

findings.title: Restore the same-repository guard for self-hosted execution
findings.whyItBreaks: Fork pull requests now reach this self-hosted job. The workflow checks out the attacker-controlled head SHA, installs its dependency graph, and executes its `src/cli.ts`, allowing a fork author to run arbitrary commands on the self-hosted runner.

findings.title: Keep fork PRs off the self-hosted runner
findings.whyItBreaks: The removed job-level condition previously limited execution to same-repository PRs. Fork PRs now reach the checkout of their head SHA and execute PR-controlled dependency installation and `src/cli.ts` on the self-hosted runner, allowing an external contributor to run arbitrary code in that runner environment.

findings.title: Preserve zero-duration configuration
findings.whyItBreaks: Previously, setting NEEDLEFISH_RUNNER_TIMEOUT_GRACE_MS=0 or NEEDLEFISH_RUNNER_SIGKILL_GIVE_UP_MS=0 produced a zero-delay timer. This change silently replaces either configured value with the 5000 ms or 2000 ms fallback, so deployments relying on immediate SIGKILL escalation or immediate give-up now wait seconds longer after a runner timeout.

findings.title: Keep zero as a supported runner delay
findings.whyItBreaks: Before this change, setting either runner-delay environment variable to "0" produced an immediate timer. The new predicate converts that explicit configuration to the fallback (5,000 ms for SIGTERM grace or 2,000 ms for SIGKILL give-up), so existing deployments that disable either wait now silently wait seconds instead.

findings.title: Preserve zero-duration runner configuration
findings.whyItBreaks: Previously, setting either runner-duration environment variable to "0" was accepted and passed to setTimeout, allowing immediate escalation. This change instead substitutes the defaults: 5,000 ms before SIGKILL and 2,000 ms before giving up. Existing deployments using zero for immediate cleanup will now wait up to seven seconds after the main timeout.

findings.title: Restore deep-review coverage for every changed file
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed line limits deep review to six mapped hotspots. Removing the tail-coverage hotspot means any changed file not included in those selected hotspots receives no deep-review pass, so defects in that file can be omitted from the candidate review.

findings.title: Propagate residual risks from deep reviews
findings.whyItBreaks: Successful deep reviews can return residual_risks, but this changed assignment always replaces them with []. The critic receives no blocking-risk context, and deriveVerdict at src/core/review.ts:122 therefore only sees residuals the critic independently emits.

findings.title: Restore deep coverage for unselected changed files
findings.whyItBreaks: The new top-six hotspot selection is now the entire deep-review set. Changed files not included in those selected hotspots are never sent to the deep-review loop, allowing large reviews to return clean without reviewing every changed file.

findings.title: Preserve residual risks from deep reviews
findings.whyItBreaks: Successful deep reviews can return blocking residual risks, but this changed assignment replaces all of them with an empty list before the critic prompt is built. The critic and final verdict therefore lose unresolved blockers reported by deep review.

findings.title: Deep-review every changed file
findings.whyItBreaks: Large bundles can contain more than six independently mapped hotspots. `slice(0, MAX_HOTSPOTS)` drops every lower-ranked hotspot, and removing the tail-coverage hotspot means their changed files receive no deep review. The critic then receives only a patch stat, not the omitted diff, so it can return a clean verdict without inspecting those files.

findings.title: Preserve deep-pass residual risks for the verdict
findings.whyItBreaks: Each deep pass can return blocking residual risks when it cannot establish a safe verdict. This change replaces their aggregate with `[]`, so the critic receives no evidence that a hotspot was unresolved and `deriveVerdict` can receive a PASS-compatible result rather than the prior blocking residual.

findings.title: Preserve focus in large-review deep prompts
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed large-review path removes the only focus substitution while small reviews still serialize the full bundle. Consequently, caller-provided focus no longer reaches deep-review calls for large patches.

findings.title: Preserve focus in large-review prompts
findings.whyItBreaks: The large-review path no longer passes bundle.focus to map.md or replaces {{FOCUS}} for deep.md. A caller requesting a focused review of a large patch therefore receives an unrestricted hotspot review instead.

findings.title: Forward focus and deep options to the large-review map prompt
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed mapBundle omits bundle.focus and bundle.deep, so reviews routed through reviewLarge no longer pass those caller-selected options to the map pass. This changes large-review behavior while small reviews still serialize the complete Bundle.

findings.title: Carry rounded seconds into the next minute
findings.whyItBreaks: For 119500ms, the changed calculation derives 1 minute then rounds the remainder to 60 seconds, rendering "1m 60s" instead of "2m 0s".

findings.title: Normalize seconds after rounding
findings.whyItBreaks: For 119500 ms, minutes is floored to 1 while the remaining seconds round to 60, so stats render as `1m 60s` instead of the correct `2m 0s`.

findings.title: Normalize rounded seconds into minutes
findings.whyItBreaks: Minutes are calculated before the remaining seconds are rounded. Consequently, 59,500ms renders as `60s` and 119,500ms as `1m 60s`, rather than carrying the rounded second into minutes.

findings.title: Reject zero in parsePositiveInteger
findings.whyItBreaks: parsePositiveInteger promises a positive integer, but 0 now passes and is returned. Public callers can therefore receive zero where the API guarantees a value greater than zero.

findings.title: Reject zero to preserve the positive-integer contract
findings.whyItBreaks: `parsePositiveInteger("0", label)` now returns `0` because `0 < 0` is false, despite the exported function name and error text promising a positive integer. Public callers can rely on that promise when using the result for counts, limits, or attempts.

findings.title: Reject zero to preserve the positive-integer contract
findings.whyItBreaks: `parsePositiveInteger` and its error text promise a positive integer, but the changed guard accepts `0` and returns it to callers. This public API now violates its stated result contract.
